facts.text: Smiling can actually make you feel happier, even if you're faking it. Your brain doesn't differentiate between a real and forced smile!
The first computer programmer was a woman named Ada Lovelace, who wrote the first algorithm for Charles Babbage's Analytical Engine in the 1840s.
Your brain generates enough electricity to power a small light bulb. That's why great ideas are often represented by a light bulb!
The act of helping others has been shown to increase happiness levels more than spending money on yourself.
The first website ever created is still online! You can visit it at info.cern.ch.
Your brain processes images 60,000 times faster than text, which is why visual portfolios can be so effective!
Taking short breaks during work actually increases productivity rather than decreasing it.
The term 'bug' in computing originated when a moth got trapped in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947, causing it to malfunction.
Learning new skills creates new neural pathways in your brain, keeping it young and adaptable.
The 'flow state' (being completely absorbed in a challenging task) is one of the most satisfying psychological states humans can experience.
The first computer mouse was made of wood and invented by Doug Engelbart in 1964.
Your brain is more active during sleep than during the day, consolidating memories and processing emotions.
Spending time in nature for just 20 minutes can significantly reduce stress hormone levels.
The entire Apollo 11 mission that landed humans on the moon used less computing power than a modern smartphone.
Your brain can't actually multitask - it switches between tasks very quickly, giving the illusion of doing multiple things at once.
Expressing gratitude regularly has been linked to improved mental health and stronger relationships.
The first email was sent by Ray Tomlinson in 1971, and the message was simply 'QWERTYUIOP'.
Your brain continues to develop new neurons throughout your life, a process called neurogenesis.
The 'spotlight effect' means we overestimate how much others notice our mistakes - they're usually too focused on themselves!
The first domain name ever registered was symbolics.com on March 15, 1985.
The human brain can store approximately 2.5 petabytes of information - equivalent to 3 million hours of TV shows!
Laughter is contagious because of mirror neurons in our brains that activate when we see others experiencing emotions.
The first tweet was sent by Jack Dorsey on March 21, 2006, and it said 'just setting up my twttr'.
Your brain uses about 20% of your body's total energy, despite being only 2% of your body weight.
The 'Tetris Effect' occurs when people devote so much time to an activity that it begins to pattern their thoughts and dreams.
The first YouTube video was uploaded on April 23, 2005, titled 'Me at the zoo' and is still viewable today.
The human brain has approximately 86 billion neurons, each connected to thousands of other neurons.
The 'Ikea Effect' is a cognitive bias where people place higher value on things they partially created themselves.
The first smartphone, called the Simon Personal Communicator, was created by IBM in 1992.
Your brain can't feel pain - surgeons can operate on a brain while the patient is awake!
The 'Google Effect' shows that we're less likely to remember information we know we can easily find online.
The first text message was sent on December 3, 1992, and it said 'Merry Christmas'.
The brain's 'default mode network' becomes active when you're daydreaming, and it's crucial for creativity.
The 'Dunning-Kruger Effect' is when people with limited knowledge overestimate their abilities, while experts tend to underestimate theirs.
The first digital photograph was taken in 1957, almost 20 years before the invention of the first digital camera.
Your brain activity is as unique as your fingerprint - neuroscientists can identify you based on your brain waves.
The 'Zeigarnik Effect' means we remember uncompleted tasks better than completed ones - that's why cliffhangers work!
The first mobile phone call was made by Martin Cooper of Motorola on April 3, 1973, to his rival at Bell Labs.
Your brain's storage capacity is considered virtually unlimited - you're not likely to 'run out of space' for memories.
The 'Baader-Meinhof Phenomenon' occurs when something you recently learned suddenly appears everywhere - it's an illusion!
The first commercially available computer game was 'Pong,' released by Atari in 1972.
Your brain can generate about 23 watts of power when you're awake - enough to power a low-energy LED light.
The 'Pratfall Effect' shows that making small mistakes can actually make you more likable and relatable to others.
The first social media site was Six Degrees, launched in 1997, allowing users to create profiles and list friends.
Your brain processes about 11 million bits of information every second, but you're only consciously aware of about 50 bits.
The 'Mere Exposure Effect' means we tend to like things more simply because we're familiar with them.
The first emoji was created in 1999 by Shigetaka Kurita in Japan, and the original set had just 176 symbols.
Your brain's long-term memory storage is theoretically unlimited in capacity and duration.
The 'Peak-End Rule' shows we judge experiences based on how they felt at their peak and at their end, not the average of the whole experience.
The first version of HTML had only 18 elements, compared to over 100 in modern HTML5.
